# Core Thesis

Manufactured traction is not merely fake popularity. It is the systematic corruption of the signals that digital markets use to allocate attention, trust, money, and legitimacy.

In social media, it appears as fake engagement, clipping networks, creator farms, engagement pods, and trend simulation. In commerce, it appears as fake reviews, edited testimonials, hidden sponsorships, and trust-badge laundering. In app stores, it appears as manipulated ratings, reviews, installs, charts, and search placement. In advertising, it appears as invalid traffic, fake leads, and conversion noise. In attribution, it appears as click flooding, install hijacking, cookie stuffing, and last-click capture. In music and media, it appears as fake streams, AI-content flooding, playlist manipulation, podcast download inflation, and synthetic influencers. In startups, it appears as ARR inflation, launch-rank gaming, compliance theater, and inflated community metrics.

The shared ethical problem is that people and algorithms are made to reward evidence that has been strategically distorted.

## Academic version

Manufactured traction is the strategic distortion, amplification, or concealment of visibility, credibility, conversion, attribution, and legitimacy signals so that platforms, audiences, customers, or investors infer genuine demand where the underlying evidence has been engineered. Its ethical harm is not simply that individual metrics are fake, but that the systems responsible for allocating attention, trust, and money are made to reward distorted proof.

## Why the thesis is stronger than “fake engagement”

“Fake engagement” captures only one symptom. The broader issue is proxy corruption. Digital markets use imperfect proxies because direct truth is expensive:

- People use reviews because they cannot test every product.
- Platforms use engagement because they cannot directly observe value.
- Investors use ARR because they cannot fully inspect future viability.
- App stores use ratings and installs because they need scalable ranking signals.
- Search engines use content/reputation signals because they cannot perfectly judge usefulness.

Manufactured traction exploits those shortcuts.
